questions.text: Change the following narration The customer said to the shopkeeper,"What is the price of this article?"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The customer asked the shopkeeper what the price of this article was.
options.explanation: The word order is incorrect in this option. The correct order is "asked the shopkeeper what the price of that article was."

options.text: The customer asked the shopkeeper that what the price of that article was.
options.explanation: The word order is incorrect in this option. The correct order is "asked the shopkeeper what the price of that article was."

options.text: The customer asked the shopkeeper what the price of that article was.
options.explanation: In reported speech, when changing direct speech into indirect speech, the verb tense and pronouns often need to be adjusted. In this case, "said" changes to "asked," and "is" changes to "was." The correct choice maintains the correct tense and structure.

options.text: The customer asked the shopkeeper what was the price of that article.
options.explanation: This option changes the word order and places the subject "the price" before the verb "was," which is not the correct structure.

questions.explanation: In reported speech, when changing direct speech into indirect speech, the verb tense and pronouns often need to be adjusted. In this case, "said" changes to "asked," and "is" changes to "was." The correct choice maintains the correct tense and structure.

questions.text: Change the following narration The teacher said,"Boys,what are you doing here?"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The teacher asked the boys what they are doing there.
options.explanation: The verb tense should be changed to the past tense ("were") to match the indirect speech.

options.text: The teacher asked the boys what they were doing there.
options.explanation: Similar to the previous question, when converting direct speech to indirect speech, the verb tenses need to be adjusted. "Are" changes to "were" to match the past tense of reporting.

options.text: The teacher asked the boys what they were doing here.
options.explanation: This option maintains the past tense in the reported speech but changes "there" to "here," which is incorrect.

options.text: The teacher asked the boys what were they doing there.
options.explanation: The correct word order should be "what they were doing there," not "what were they doing there."

questions.explanation: Similar to the previous question, when converting direct speech to indirect speech, the verb tenses need to be adjusted. "Are" changes to "were" to match the past tense of reporting.

questions.text: Change the following narration His aunt said,"What a pleasant surprise to see you here!"
questions.subject: English

options.text: His aunt exclaimed that it was a very pleasant surprise to see him there.
options.explanation: The verb "said" changes to "exclaimed," and the pronouns and tense are adjusted to fit indirect speech.

options.text: His aunt exclaimed that it was a very pleasant surprise to see you there.
options.explanation: This option uses "see you there" instead of "see him there," which is incorrect.

options.text: His aunt exclaimed that it was a very pleasant surprise to see him there.
options.explanation: This is the correct answer.

options.text: His aunt exclaimed that it was a very pleasant surprise to seeing him there.
options.explanation: The use of "seeing" is incorrect. The correct form is "to see."

questions.explanation: His aunt exclaimed that it was a very pleasant surprise to see him there. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: Change the following narrationThe cobbler said,"How stupid I am!"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The cobbler exclaimed that he was very stupid.

options.text: The cobbler exclaimed that he is very stupid.

options.text: The cobbler exclaimed that I was a very stupid.

questions.text: Change the following narrationThe captain said, "Good morning, Friends!"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The captain said his friends good morning.
options.explanation: The use of "said" is not as appropriate as "bade" in this context.

options.text: The captain bade his friends good morning.
options.explanation: "Bade" is a more appropriate verb to use when conveying a greeting.

options.text: The captain said to his friends, good morning friends.
options.explanation: The repetition of "good morning friends" is not necessary in indirect speech.

options.text: The captain said to his friends good morning.
options.explanation: Similar to the previous option, the repetition of "good morning" is not necessary.

questions.explanation: "Bade" is a more appropriate verb to use when conveying a greeting.

questions.text: Change the following narrationThe boy said,"If I were a king!"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The boy wished that he were a king.
options.explanation: The pronoun "I" should be changed to "he" to correctly attribute the statement to the boy. Additionally, the tense should be "had been" instead of "was."

options.text: The boy wished that I was a king.
options.explanation: The tense "has been" is not appropriate here. The correct form is "had been."

options.text: The boy wished that he had been a king.
options.explanation: The verb "said" changes to "wished," and the tense is adjusted to reflect the past condition.

options.text: The boy wished that he has been a king.
options.explanation: The tense "is" is not appropriate here. The correct form is "had been."

questions.text: Change the following narrationThe father said to his sons,"Lets us go out for a walk."

options.text: The father suggested to his that we should go out for a walk.

questions.text: The doctor said, "Do not miss the medicine"
questions.subject: English

options.text: The doctor told to miss the medicine
options.explanation: Incorrect verb usage, "told" doesn't convey the same meaning as "forbade"

options.text: The doctor forbade not to miss the medicine
options.explanation: Incorrect negation, "forbade not to" is a double negative

options.text: The doctor asked to miss the medicine
options.explanation: Incorrect verb usage, "asked" doesn't convey the same meaning as "forbade"

options.text: The doctor forbade to miss the medicine.
options.explanation: The original sentence is a direct speech, and when we convert it to indirect speech, we use the verb "forbade" to indicate that the doctor prohibited or disallowed something.

questions.explanation: The original sentence is a direct speech, and when we convert it to indirect speech, we use the verb "forbade" to indicate that the doctor prohibited or disallowed something.
